citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee has a formal arrangement with an attending veterinarian and a written Program of Veterinary Care but does
not have arrangements for the Attending Veterinarian (AV) to conduct regularly scheduled visits to the premises. The
licensee provided a Program of Veterinary Care with the space documenting the frequency of the regularly scheduled
visits by the veterinarian that states “in office as needed”. The licensee could not provide specific information about any
regularly scheduled visits to the facility by the Attending Veterinarian but stated it has been at least 1.5 years since the
last visit to the facility. APHIS inspectors called the AV’s office and spoke with a clinic representative who could not
provide any information regarding a site visit to the facility in at least the last two years.
Without the required regularly scheduled visits to the premises, the AV cannot effectively ensure the provision of
adequate veterinary care, and the health and well-being of the animals housed at the facility.
Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor.
Correct by 1 July 2026

The facility has not adequately maintained records for all animal dispositions. The facility did not create a record for the
death of a black-backed jackal that passed in July 2025.
Disposition records are necessary for accurate tracking of animals used in regulated activities to ensure their proper care,
humane transportation, and to be able to track and trace animals in the event of an animal disease outbreak or loss/theft.
Dealers shall make, keep, and maintain records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the following information
concerning animals other than dogs and cats, purchased or otherwise acquired, owned, held, leased, or otherwise in his
or her possession or under his or her control, or which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that
dealer or exhibitor: the date of purchase, acquisition, sale, or disposal of the animal(s).
Correct by 1 June 2026 and going forward

The facility is not adequately maintaining housekeeping of the premises. There is unkept and heavy overgrowth of
vegetation surrounding the perimeter of two outdoor primary enclosures. An enclosure housing a black-backed jackal has
dense vegetation growing around and over the primary enclosure. The dense vegetation obscures a clear view of the
jackal and the interior of the enclosure. The second primary enclosure housing 2 black-backed jackals has dense
vegetation growing up the sides and on top of the enclosure fencing. The weight of the vegetation on top of the enclosure
is causing the fencing to bow inwards towards the inside of the enclosure.
Overgrowth of plants, weeds, and other types of vegetation can prevent proper cleaning of enclosures, reduce the
effectiveness of daily animal observations, and serve as harborage for pests and insects. Additionally, overgrowth of
plants on the sides and top of the enclosure can damage the fencing and allow for potential escape of the animals being
housed in this enclosure.
Premises (buildings and grounds) shall be kept clean and in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to facilitate
the prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart.
Correct by 15 June 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.40(a)(1) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee has a formal arrangement with an attending veterinarian and a written Program of Veterinary Care but does 
not have arrangements for the Attending Veterinarian (AV) to conduct regularly scheduled visits to the premises. The 
licensee provided a Program of Veterinary Care with the space documenting the frequency of the regularly scheduled 
visits by the veterinarian that states “in office as needed”. The licensee could not provide specific information about any 
regularly scheduled visits to the facility by the Attending Veterinarian but stated it has been at least 1.5 years since the 
last visit to the facility. APHIS inspectors called the AV’s office and spoke with a clinic representative who could not 
provide any information regarding a site visit to the facility in at least the last two years. 
 
Without the required regularly scheduled visits to the premises, the AV cannot effectively ensure the provision of 
adequate veterinary care, and the health and well-being of the animals housed at the facility. 
 
Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time 
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary 
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor. 
Correct by 1 July 2026 
2.75(b)(1)(v) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
The facility has not adequately maintained records for all animal dispositions. The facility did not create a record for the 
death of a black-backed jackal that passed in July 2025.  
 
Disposition records are necessary for accurate tracking of animals used in regulated activities to ensure their proper care, 
humane transportation, and to be able to track and trace animals in the event of an animal disease outbreak or loss/theft. 
 
Dealers shall make, keep, and maintain records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the following information 
concerning animals other than dogs and cats, purchased or otherwise acquired, owned, held, leased, or otherwise in his 
or her possession or under his or her control, or which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that 
dealer or exhibitor: the date of purchase, acquisition, sale, or disposal of the animal(s). 
Correct by 1 June 2026 and going forward

3.131(c) 
   
Sanitation. 
The facility is not adequately maintaining housekeeping of the premises. There is unkept and heavy overgrowth of 
vegetation surrounding the perimeter of two outdoor primary enclosures. An enclosure housing a black-backed jackal has 
dense vegetation growing around and over the primary enclosure. The dense vegetation obscures a clear view of the 
jackal and the interior of the enclosure. The second primary enclosure housing 2 black-backed jackals has dense 
vegetation growing up the sides and on top of the enclosure fencing. The weight of the vegetation on top of the enclosure 
is causing the fencing to bow inwards towards the inside of the enclosure.  
 
Overgrowth of plants, weeds, and other types of vegetation can prevent proper cleaning of enclosures, reduce the 
effectiveness of daily animal observations, and serve as harborage for pests and insects. Additionally, overgrowth of 
plants on the sides and top of the enclosure can damage the fencing and allow for potential escape of the animals being 
housed in this enclosure.  
 
Premises (buildings and grounds) shall be kept clean and in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to facilitate 
the prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. 
Correct by 15 June 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
